input.new_string: ## Appendix: Eventual Consistency and the Missing Completion Contract Cloud IAM is *eventually consistent*: when you change who-can-do-what, the change does not take effect everywhere at once — it propagates across replicas over seconds, occasionally minutes. For a system that is read on essentially every API call across the globe, choosing fast, always-available reads over instantly-consistent ones is a defensible engineering tradeoff, and we grant it without complaint. What is not defensible is the part the term quietly smuggles in: when a *mutating* call returns, it tells you nothing about whether the work is finished. Google Cloud's [service account](#Governor) delete returns an empty success with no operation handle — no "done" flag, no terminal state, no signal you can poll to learn that the deletion has actually settled. Google's own API design guidelines define exactly such a long-running-operation contract, and Google ships it on plenty of other services; it is simply not wired to the operations that most need it. The capability exists. The will to apply it here does not. It is worse than a merely missing signal, because a deleted service account is not actually deleted. It is *soft-deleted* and remains recoverable for thirty days. So the honest question — "is it gone yet; can I reuse the name?" — has no clean answer at all: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse. You are left to guess. The result is that every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to paper over a contract the vendor declined to write. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) does this in its [Divest](#Divest) path: after deleting a service account it polls the read path and treats the account as durably gone only after several *consecutive* "not found" reads, bounded by a timeout — debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first one. It is a workaround, and we name it as one. Plainly: this is not a hard distributed-systems problem. It is a completion contract the vendor chose not to provide, dressed in distributed-systems vocabulary. "Eventual consistency" accurately describes the read path; here it is also a polite way of saying *we will not tell you when we are finished*. We depend on Google Cloud and expect to keep depending on it — and this is still crappy engineering on exactly the surfaces where getting it right would cost them nothing in their consistency model. **If you hit it:** an operation that fails with "already exists" immediately after you deleted the same-named resource is almost always this flap, not stale local state — wait a few seconds and retry the [Divest](#Divest) or re-create before hunting for leftover state. ## Appendix: Roadmap

+You are left to guess.

[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is built on cloud APIs, and cloud APIs are *eventually consistent*: when you mutate state — grant a role, delete a [service account](#Governor), link billing — the change does not take effect everywhere at once.
It propagates across replicas over seconds, occasionally minutes.
For systems that are read on essentially every API call across the globe, choosing fast, always-available reads over instantly-consistent ones is a defensible engineering tradeoff, and we grant it without complaint.

The defensible part is the consistency *model*.
The indefensible part is what the term quietly omits: a **completion contract**.
When a mutating call returns, it tells you nothing about whether the work is finished — there is no terminal-state signal you can poll to learn that the change has settled.
This is not a law of physics.
The pattern for providing it is well understood and widely shipped: Google's own API design guidelines define long-running operations with a `done` flag and a terminal state, and Azure's Resource Manager specifies an async-operation contract end to end.
The giants deliver it excellently in places — and then withhold it on exactly the operations that race: IAM propagation, billing linkage, identity lifecycle.
The capability exists; it is selectively absent where it would matter most.

Without a completion contract, every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to compensate.
The honest version of that scaffolding polls for an *observable terminal state*; the dishonest version, which the gap quietly encourages, is a blind `sleep N` — a guessed magic number standing in for a signal that should have existed.
[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) holds the honest line where it can: it polls for the real state, requires *consecutive* confirming reads before believing a transition (debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first answer), and bounds every wait with a timeout so a never-settling operation fails loudly instead of hanging.

The sharpest instance is service-account deletion.
The delete returns an empty success with no operation handle, and the account is not actually removed — it is *soft-deleted*, recoverable for thirty days.
So even the simplest question, "is it gone; can I reuse the name?", has no clean answer: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse.
[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Divest](#Divest) path copes by treating the account as durably gone only after several consecutive "not found" reads — see the ["already exists" after a delete](#StaleDeleteRead) diagnostic for the symptom this produces.

Plainly: this is not a hard distributed-systems problem.
It is a completion contract the vendors chose not to provide, dressed in distributed-systems vocabulary.
"Eventual consistency" accurately describes the read path; here it is also a polite way of saying *we will not tell you when we are finished*.
We depend on Google Cloud and expect to keep depending on it — and building atop eventually-consistent APIs with no completion contract is still crappy engineering on the vendor's part, on exactly the surfaces where getting it right would cost them nothing in their consistency model.

-It is worse than a merely missing signal, because a deleted service account is not actually deleted.
-It is *soft-deleted* and remains recoverable for thirty days.

-So the honest question — "is it gone yet; can I reuse the name?" — has no clean answer at all: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse.
-You are left to guess.
+Without a completion contract, every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to compensate.

+The honest version of that scaffolding polls for an *observable terminal state*; the dishonest version, which the gap quietly encourages, is a blind `sleep N` — a guessed magic number standing in for a signal that should have existed.

+[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) holds the honest line where it can: it polls for the real state, requires *consecutive* confirming reads before believing a transition (debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first answer), and bounds every wait with a timeout so a never-settling operation fails loudly instead of hanging.
-The result is that every consumer independently reinvents the same retry-poll-tolerate scaffolding to paper over a contract the vendor declined to write.

-[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) does this in its [Divest](#Divest) path: after deleting a service account it polls the read path and treats the account as durably gone only after several *consecutive* "not found" reads, bounded by a timeout — debouncing the flap rather than trusting the first one.
-It is a workaround, and we name it as one.
+The sharpest instance is service-account deletion.
+The delete returns an empty success with no operation handle, and the account is not actually removed — it is *soft-deleted*, recoverable for thirty days.

+So even the simplest question, "is it gone; can I reuse the name?", has no clean answer: a read of the just-deleted account flaps between "present" and "absent" across replicas while the tombstone propagates, and no API will tell you when the name is safe to reuse.

Plainly: this is not a hard distributed-systems problem.
-It is a completion contract the vendor chose not to provide, dressed in distributed-systems vocabulary.

-We depend on Google Cloud and expect to keep depending on it — and this is still crappy engineering on exactly the surfaces where getting it right would cost them nothing in their consistency model.

# Process LINK markers
  local z_link_pattern="${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_LINK_URL}([^${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_LINK_TEXT}]*)${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_LINK_TEXT}([^${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_END}]*)${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_END}"
  while [[ "${z_str}" =~ ${z_link_pattern} ]]; do
    local z_link_url="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
    local z_link_text="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
    local z_link_full="${BASH_REMATCH[0]}"
    local z_link_replacement=""
    if test "${ZBUYM_USE_HYPERLINKS}" = "1"; then
      z_link_replacement="${BUYC_LINK}${ZBUYM_ESC}]8;;${z_link_url}${ZBUYM_ESC}\\${z_link_text}${ZBUYM_ESC}]8;;${ZBUYM_ESC}\\${BUYC_RESET}${z_ambient}"
    elif test -n "${BUYC_LINK}"; then
      z_link_replacement="${BUYC_LINK}${z_link_text}${BUYC_RESET}${z_ambient} <${z_link_url}>"
    else
      z_link_replacement="${z_link_text}"
    fi
    z_str="${z_str/${z_link_full}/${z_link_replacement}}"
  done

  # --- Resolve simple markers ---
  z_str="${z_str//${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_CMD}/${BUYC_CYAN}}"
  z_str="${z_str//${ZBUYM_DIASTEMA_UI}/${BUYC_MAGENTA}}"

Verified — the hint renders correctly. The link yawp resolves three ways: a clickable OSC-8 link on capable terminals, `GCP's post-delete read flap <URL>` (URL visible) on color-but-not-hyperlink terminals, and bare display text in the stripped logfile copy. So an operator hitting the error in their terminal always gets something navigable; only the plain log loses the URL. Good enough by design.

## What's in the draft

- **`README.md`** — new `## Appendix: Eventual Consistency and the Missing Completion Contract` (anchor `EventualConsistency`), now written top-down: the general problem of building atop eventually-consistent APIs *without a completion contract*, the fair concession (the consistency model is defensible; the missing pollable terminal-state signal is the unforced error), how Recipe Bottle copes in general (poll real state, consecutive confirms, bounded timeout — vs. the blind-`sleep N` fingerprint), and service-account delete demoted to "the sharpest instance." Plus a `StaleDeleteRead` Diagnostics entry that cross-links to it. "Crappy engineering" lands once, the rest is dry concession.
- **`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`** — the preflight `already exists` die now appends a `buyy_link_yawp` hypothesis: *"Maybe this is GCP's post-delete read flap …"* linking to the README anchor. Shellcheck clean.
- **`RBSCIP-IamPropagation.adoc`** — wove finding #2 into the delete-side flap entry: the soft-delete tombstone mechanism + the no-LRO/AIP-151-unused fact.

## Concerns

1. **Release-timing coupling on the live link.** `RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL` points at the *published* README ("updated per-release or per-incorporation"). The `#EventualConsistency` anchor only resolves once this README ships to that target — until the next release/incorporation, the error-printout link points at a stale published README missing the anchor. Not a code bug, but the hint isn't live the instant you save the file.

2. **The hypothesis also fires on genuine drift.** That 200 die legitimately triggers when a *real* leftover SA exists (the intended fail-loud case), not only the flap. I kept the original "run the matching GovernorDivests verb" guidance first and the "maybe this is the flap, wait and retry" second — so both paths are covered and the hedge is honest — but check the ordering reads right to you; it now nudges toward "wait and retry," which is wrong if it's true drift.

3. **Weave scope.** I folded finding #2 into RBSCIP only (the durable catalog). The Axis-B hyperscaler memo's GCP row could take the same soft-delete/no-LRO sharpening, and the canonical-divest evidence memo could note it — I held off to avoid sprawl. Say the word if you want it echoed there.

4. **Tone check.** One "crappy engineering," everything else conceding-but-dry. Easy to dial that phrase up or down if it's off.

5. **Not committed.** All working-tree only. The two files dirty at session start (`rbfd`, `rbgjv03`) aren't mine — I touched only `README.md`, `RBSCIP`, `rbgg_Governor.sh`. I'll notch just those three when you say.

  Evaluate refactoring RBRA from one-file-per-role-type (director, retriever — each with its
  own cult verbs) to one-file-per-person carrying any combination of roles. Collapses the
  role-typed verb surface and aligns the keyfile tier with the federation model's
  principal -> capability-set shape (smoother eventual migration).
  
  The lifecycle split is its enabler: once identity (establish) and credential (re-key) are
  separated, "a principal with a role-set" is the natural next shape.
  
  Key question to resolve: does per-person multi-role preserve least-privilege? Today's
  role-typed SAs enforce it by construction — a retriever cannot act as director. A per-person
  SA is only as least-privilege as the grants chosen; show the security guarantee survives,
  not just the convenience.
  
  Source: the federation footnote in `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`;
  `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.
  
  Done: a decision/recommendation (do / don't / how), not implementation.
